questions.questionText: The most reliable method to confirm pregnancy in the first trimester is:
questions.optionB: Transvaginal ultrasound (TVS — can detect gestational sac by 4.5-5 weeks, cardiac activity by 6-7 weeks)
questions.explanation: TVS: most reliable early pregnancy confirmation. Gestational sac visible by 4.5-5 weeks, yolk sac by 5-5.5 weeks, fetal pole with cardiac activity by 6-7 weeks. β-hCG: positive in urine (25 mIU/mL) and serum (5 mIU/mL). Doubles every 48-72h in normal pregnancy. Discriminatory level: β-hCG ~1500-2000 → should see gestational sac on TVS (ectopic if absent). Clinical signs: Hegar's sign (softening of isthmus), Chadwick's sign (bluish vagina), Goodell's sign (cervical softening). Reference: DC Dutta — Diagnosis of Pregnancy.

questions.questionText: Ectopic pregnancy most commonly occurs in:
questions.optionB: Fallopian tube — ampulla (70% of ectopic pregnancies, most common site)
questions.explanation: Ectopic pregnancy: implantation outside uterine cavity. Tubal: 95% (ampulla 70% — most common, isthmus 12%, fimbrial 11%, interstitial/cornual 2-3%). Risk factors: PID/salpingitis, previous ectopic, tubal surgery, IUD (increases ectopic risk relative to intrauterine), endometriosis, assisted reproduction. Triad: amenorrhea, abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding. Ruptured ectopic: surgical emergency (hemoperitoneum, shock). Treatment: methotrexate (unruptured, β-hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity) or surgery (salpingectomy/salpingotomy). Reference: DC Dutta — Ectopic Pregnancy.

questions.questionText: Pre-eclampsia is defined as new-onset hypertension (≥140/90) after 20 weeks of gestation with:
questions.optionB: Proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h) OR end-organ dysfunction (thrombocytopenia, renal insufficiency, liver impairment, cerebral/visual symptoms)
questions.explanation: Pre-eclampsia: pregnancy-specific syndrome. Criteria: new-onset HTN (≥140/90 on 2 occasions 4h apart) after 20 weeks + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h or PCR ≥0.3) OR end-organ damage. Severe features: BP ≥160/110, platelets <100,000, creatinine >1.1, elevated LFTs (2x), cerebral/visual symptoms, pulmonary edema. HELLP syndrome: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets. Eclampsia: pre-eclampsia + seizures. Treatment: delivery (definitive), MgSO4 (seizure prophylaxis — Pritchard/Zuspan regimen), labetalol/nifedipine (BP). Reference: DC Dutta — Pre-eclampsia.

questions.questionText: Magnesium sulfate is used in eclampsia because it acts as:
questions.optionB: Anticonvulsant (blocks NMDA receptors and calcium channels — prevents and controls eclamptic seizures, drug of choice)
questions.explanation: MgSO4: drug of choice for eclamptic seizures (both prevention and treatment). Mechanism: NMDA receptor antagonist, calcium channel blocker, cerebral vasodilator. Pritchard regimen: loading 4g IV + 10g IM (5g each buttock), maintenance 5g IM every 4h. Zuspan regimen: loading 4g IV, maintenance 1-2g/h IV infusion. Monitoring: knee jerk (first to disappear), respiratory rate (>16/min), urine output (>25mL/h). Toxicity: loss of reflexes → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest. Antidote: calcium gluconate 10% IV. Reference: DC Dutta — Eclampsia MgSO4.
